segments.s142-g25: بیامد is a simple past intransitive verb 'came' and appears before its noun (verb–subject order, common in poetic Persian). دمان 'the hem/skirt' is the (grammatical) subject; پیش is an adverbial 'forward/in front'; گرد آفرید is a light-noun + verb sequence where گرد ('a round/around' used as a nominal element) combines with آفرید 'made/created' to mean 'formed/made a circle' or 'wheeled about.'
segments.s142-g26: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' دخت is 'maiden' modified by the compound adjective/participle کمندافگن 'bearing or skilled with a lasso' (postnominal modifier). او را is the pronoun 'him/her' with the object marker را (marks a definite direct object), and بدید is the past tense 'saw.' The clause is a subordinate time clause tied to the previous main clause.
segments.s142-g27: کمان را marks 'the bow' with the definite object marker را. The verbal cluster بزه کرد is poetic/archaic: کرد is the main past-tense verb 'did/made' while بزه is a verbal/noun element whose precise archaic hunting sense is not among the listed senses (hence not matched to them). و introduces the next verb phrase; بگشاد (a past form of گشودن) means 'opened/drew (it)' or 'spread' here, and بر is a preposition 'toward/upon' linking to what follows.
segments.s142-g28: نبد is an archaic negative/existential form (parallel to نبود) meaning 'there was not' or 'did not remain.' مرغ را is 'the bird' with the object marker; پیش is 'before/in front of' and تیرش is 'his arrow' (تیر + enclitic possessive ش). گذر is a noun 'passage/passing' — together: 'there was no bird before the passage of his arrow.'
segments.s142-g29: به سهراب is a prepositional phrase 'upon Sohrab' (marking the target). بر تیر باران is a compound where تیر باران literally 'arrow‑rain' (باران used figuratively) describes a shower of arrows; گرفت is the past tense 'took/ seized' used idiomatically here as 'he began/let fall' or 'he showered' (i.e. 'he laid a rain of arrows on Sohrab').
segments.s142-g30: چپ و راست 'left and right' is an idiom meaning 'everywhere/on both flanks.' جنگ سواران is a noun phrase 'the fighting's horsemen' or 'horsemen in the battle' (جنگ as noun 'battle/fight' modifying سواران). گرفت repeats the past verb 'took/struck' (here: horsemen were hit/struck left and right). Word order places the adverbial phrase up front and the verb at the end, typical for emphasis in poetry.
segments.s142-g31: نگه کرد is a compound verb 'kept watch/looked' (نگه as noun/verb element + کرد 'did'); سهراب follows as the subject. آمدش is آمد 'came' with the enclitic ش 'to him' ('came to him'), and ننگ is the noun 'shame' that is the thing coming upon him — 'shame came to him.' The object marker is not present because آمدش takes an internal noun subject.
segments.s142-g32: برآشفت is a past tense verb 'became angry/agitated.' و links the clauses. تیز is an adverbial/qualifying adjective here meaning 'swift(ly),' اندر آمد is a compound verb 'entered into' (اندر 'in' + آمد 'came'), and به جنگ completes the prepositional phrase 'into the battle/fight.' The sequence describes a sudden transition from shame to enraged action.
segments.s142-g41: برآشفت is a simple past/preterite verb (poetic form) meaning 'became angry/was enraged' and stands before the subject (verbal fronting is common in Persian poetic style). سهراب is the subject that follows the verb; و links this clause to what follows; شد + چون پلنگ forms a copular clause where شد ('became') takes a simile complement introduced by چون ('like/as') and پلنگ ('leopard') as the predicative noun.
segments.s142-g42: چو here = 'when' (temporal), introducing a subordinate/time clause. بدخواه او is a noun phrase 'his ill-wisher/enemy' (بدخواه = ill-wisher, او = 'him/he'); چاره‌گر بد is a compound nominal phrase meaning 'an evil contriver/one who devises bad schemes' (چاره‌گر literally 'solver/contriver' qualified by بد 'bad/evil'). The verb is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis): the clause implies 'when his enemy, an evil contriver, came/acted in battle'; به جنگ is the adverbial phrase 'into the battle'.
segments.s142-g43: عنان ('reins') is the direct object; برگرایید is a past verb meaning 'turned (them)' or 'wheeled' (بر + گرایید), so the literal sequence is 'he turned the reins'. و links to برگاشت اسپ, where برگاشت is the past of برگَشتن 'took up/raised' and اسپ is 'steed/horse' — 'and he lifted his horse.' Word order is verb–object (verb then noun) which is idiomatic in Persian poetry.
segments.s142-g44: بیامد is a past intransitive verb 'he came' or 'he advanced'. به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/character of' (به + کردار, کردار here = 'conduct/way'), and آذرگشسپ is a proper name used as the model of that manner. The whole clause reads 'he came in the manner of Azar-gushasp.'
segments.s142-g45: زدوده is a past participle/adjectival form ('cleansed', here functioning like 'drawn' or 'unsheathed') placed before سنان ('spear'), so 'the drawn/unsheathed spear.' آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. درربود is a past verb meaning 'seized' or 'took hold of.' The clause thus narrates consecutive actions: 'the spear (having been drawn), then he seized (it).'
segments.s142-g46: درآمد here is an intransitive past verb 'entered/came upon' or 'it came in'; بدو is the clitic pronoun 'to him' (or 'upon him'); هم = 'also' or 'too'. به کردار is the idiomatic 'in the manner of' (به + کردار), and دود = 'smoke'. So syntactically it's 'it came upon him too in the manner of smoke' — i.e., he was engulfed like smoke. Note the impersonal/experiential use of درآمد.
segments.s142-g47: بزد is a past tense verb 'he struck/smote'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and کمربند 'belt' is its object; گردآفرید (Gordafarid) is the possessor in apposition (poetic compression of 'Gordafarid's belt'). The clause literally reads 'he struck upon Gordafarid's belt.'
segments.s142-g48: زره ('armor') is the object; بر here marks location 'on' (زره بر برش = 'the armor on her'). برش in this context functions as 'upon her / its pieces' (rendered here as 'pieces' for the poetic meaning), and یک به یک is the adverbial idiom 'one by one' (یک + به + یک, where به functions idiomatically to form the distributive). بردرید is the past verb 'tore apart/cut through.' Altogether: 'he tore the armor on her apart, one piece at a time.'
segments.s142-g9: بپوشید is a past action ('he/they put on'); in older epic Persian the third-person plural can appear without -ند, so the verb precedes its patient. درع is the direct object 'armor' and سواران (horsemen/riders) is the (understood) subject following it in the line. جنگ here is attributive, specifying the riders as 'warriors' (lit. 'riders of war').
segments.s142-g10: نبود is an existential negative ('there was not' / 'there was no'); the following prepositional phrase اندر آن کار جای درنگ gives the domain where the lack obtains: 'in that matter (آن کار) there was no place (جای) for delay (درنگ)'. Word order is typical Persian: copula-like verb first, then the locative/nominal phrase.
segments.s142-g11: نهان کرد is a causative/perfective construction 'made (it) hidden' → 'hid'. The patient گیسو 'hair/lock' follows the verb, and the locative phrase به زیر زره ('underneath the armor') specifies where it was hidden. به + زیر here marks direction/location 'into/under'.
segments.s142-g12: بزد is simple past 'he struck'. The prepositional expression بر سر 'upon the head' gives the target, and the following noun phrase ترگ رومی گره is a genitival/attributive chain: 'the knotted rag of the Roman' (گره = knot). The exact referent (a knot, rag, or other object) is literary/graphic, but grammatically it's the object of بزد.
segments.s142-g13: فرود آمد is a compound verb meaning 'he descended' or 'alighted'. از دژ is the source 'from the fortress', and به کردار شیر is a simile 'in the manner/like a lion' (کردار = 'manner/conduct'). The preposition به marks the manner/comparison here.
segments.s142-g14: This line is elliptical: the verb 'to gird' (بستن) is omitted, a common poetic compression. کمر ('waist/loin') with the phrase بر میان ('in the midst of') + بادپایی ('the swift-footed') expresses 'he girded his loins among the fleet-footed', and به زیر ('below/under') locates them as below him. In short, an implied 'he girded his waist' is understood.
segments.s142-g15: به پیش سپاه is a directional phrase 'to the front of the army/host'. اندر آمد is 'entered / came in', and چو گرد is a simile 'like a dust-cloud/whirlwind' (گرد used as 'dust/cloud'). Word order places the goal/prefix phrase before آمد, as is idiomatic in Persian narrative.
segments.s142-g16: چو رعد خروشان is another simile 'like roaring thunder' (رعد = thunder, خروشان = roaring). یکی 'one' qualifies ویله (an obscure/rare noun here — probably a cry or sound), and کرد is the past verb 'he made/did' → 'he uttered/let out'. The author uses an onomatopoetic or rare word (ویله) whose precise meaning is uncertain but is best read as a loud single cry.
segments.s142-g49: ز زین برگرفتش = a compact past action: ز زین (from the saddle) + برگرفتش (برگرفت + ­ش) ‘he took/ seized it up’ — ­ش is the enclitic object marker ‘it/him’. به کردار introduces a simile/stance ‘in the manner of’ (به used adverbially), and گوی (‘sphere/ball’) names the object of the comparison; word order is topic (from the saddle) → verb → simile phrase.
segments.s142-g50: چو چوگان به زخم اندر آید بدوی = a simile clause introduced by چو ‘as/when’. چوگان modifies گوی in the preceding segment (‘the ball of polo’), به + زخم اندر آید = ‘enters into the wound’ (به…آید = ‘goes/comes into’), and بدوی is a terse, archaic/poetic verb meaning ‘(he) ran/rushed’ (here the subject continues from the previous verb).
segments.s142-g51: چو بر زین بپیچید گرد آفرید = a temporal clause beginning with چو ‘when’; بر زین بپیچید is a past intransitive verb phrase ‘he turned/wheeled upon the saddle’. گرد آفرِید here is the proper name گردآفرید written as two words (so گرد + آفرید together identify the character); the verb bپیچید governs the time reference for the next action.
segments.s142-g52: یکی تیغ تیز از میان برکشید = direct main clause: یکی (a/one, here ‘one’ or ‘a certain’) + تیغ تیز ‘a sharp sword’ is the object, and از میان برکشید is a perfective verbal phrase ‘drew (it) out from the middle/between’. برکشید is the common classical verb for drawing a weapon.
segments.s142-g53: بزد نیزه او به دو نیم کرد = compound past: بزد ‘he struck/smote’ (simple past), followed by a causative result phrase نیزه او به دو نیم کرد ‘he made his spear into two halves’ (به دو نیم marks the result/state ‘into two halves’, کرد is the light verb ‘made/did’ that yields ‘split’). Object pronoun او refers to the possessor of the spear.
segments.s142-g54: نشست از بر اسپ و برخاست گرد = two coordinated past actions: نشست از بر اسپ ‘he alighted/dismounted from the steed’ (نشست here = dismount/settle off), و برخاست ‘and he rose (up)’, with گرد as an adverbial ‘about/around’ or emphatic classical particle indicating movement ‘about/around him’. Word order: dismount clause → and → rise clause → circumstantial adverb.
segments.s142-g55: به آورد با او بسنده نبود = an evaluative clause: به آورد (infinitival/nominalized use of آورد ‘to bring’) + با او ‘with/against him’ functions as the subject/topic (‘bringing it against him’), بسنده نبود = ‘was not sufficient’ (نبود is the past copula ‘was not’). The construction treats the whole verbal idea as a noun phrase followed by ‘was not enough’.
segments.s142-g56: بپیچید ازو روی و برگاشت زود = two short actions: بپیچید ازو روی ‘he turned his face away from him’ (بپیچید = turned; ازو = away/from him; روی = face/turning of the face), و برگاشت زود ‘and he withdrew/withdrew quickly’ — برگاشت here is a classical perfective meaning ‘took away/withdrew’ used intransitively as ‘retreated’, and زود marks the quickness.
segments.s142-g57: The clause gives the actor and action: سپهبد (the spahbed/commander) is the subject, عنان (reins/control) names what is let go, اژدها را is the direct object marked by the object marker را, and سپرد is a past-tense verb 'handed/entrusted/released'. Word order is typical poetic inversion: 'reins' follows the subject and the verb completes the clause.
segments.s142-g58: به خشم is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in anger' (به used adverbially), از جهان روشنایی is a partitive/genitive-like phrase 'the light from the world', and ببرد is the verb 'to carry/take' (here rendered narratively as 'carried away'). The verb bears the old prefix بـ (common in classical Persian forms) and completes the predicate for the previous clause.
segments.s142-g59: چو آمد = 'when (it) came' (چو as a temporal 'when'), خروشان is an adverbial participle 'roaring', and به تنگ اندرش literally 'into/near the narrow (space) within him/her' (به + تنگ + اندر + -ش enclitic 'his/her'). This is a compact temporal/locative clause setting the scene.
segments.s142-g60: بجنبید is a past/remote-form meaning 'it/ she moved or was stirred', و is the conjunction 'and', برداشت here is the verb 'removed/took off' (the sense of lifting/removal), خود is a reflexive/emphatic 'its/ her own', and از سرش 'from her head' (from + head + enclitic). Together: it stirred and removed its own (covering) from her head.
segments.s142-g61: رها شد is a passive-like construction 'was freed/was released' (رها + شد), ز بند زره = 'from the bond of the armor' (ز = poetic 'from', بند = 'fetter/bond', زره = 'armor'), and موی اوی 'her hair' (موی + اوی enclitic 'her'). The structure is passive: 'her hair became freed from the armor's bonds.'
segments.s142-g62: درفشان here is adjectival 'shining/glittering', چو خورشید شد = 'became like the sun' (چو = 'like/as', شد = 'became'), and روی اوی = 'her face'. The clause is a simile: 'her (face) became shining like the sun.'
segments.s142-g63: بدانست is 'he/it knew/realized', followed by سهراب (the proper name, Sohrab) as the perceiver; کاو here functions as a connective/relative introducing the subordinate clause 'that she is a daughter' (in modern terms 'that'), and دخترست is the nominal predication 'is a daughter' (دختر + -ست copula).
segments.s142-g64: سر و موی او = 'her head and hair' (سر 'head', موی 'hair', او enclitic 'her'), ازدر is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase ('from/away from the threshold/presence' used here as 'from/underneath'), and افسرست is a nominal phrase meaning 'is a helmet' or 'was a helmet' (افسر + -ست). The whole says her head-and-hair were revealed/stood out from the helmet/covering.
segments.s142-g33: سپر بر سر آورد و بنهاد روی is two coordinated verb phrases describing successive actions. سپر بر سر آورد is literal 'he brought/put the shield on (his) head' — آورد is a past-tense perfective verb placed after its object phrase (typical Persian word order). و links the next verb phrase; بنهاد روی is literary: بنهاد (from نهاد, 'set/placed') takes روی ('face') as its object, meaning 'he fixed/turned his face' (subject is omitted and shared with the previous verb).
segments.s142-g34: This is a prepositional/locative clause with the past verb آمد at the end: ز پیگار خون اندر آمد به جوی = 'blood from the battle came into the stream.' ز marks source ('from'), اندر آمد is a poetic combination meaning 'came into / entered', and به جوی gives the direction/goal 'into the stream.'
segments.s142-g35: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction 'when'; the clause follows an object-before-verb order common in classical Persian here: سهراب را (definite object marked by را) + دید (past 'saw') + گردآفرید (the subject appearing after the verb for stylistic inversion). So the clause reads 'when Gordafarid saw Sohrab.'
segments.s142-g36: که introduces a relative clause modifying گردآفرید ('who...'). برسان آتش is literal 'bringing/with fire' (برسان 'bring/deliver' + آتش 'fire' used metaphorically as ardor). همی is the archaic/progressive particle indicating continuing or vivid action. بردمید is a past-form verb meaning 'carried/borne' (here: 'she bore/advanced bearing fire' — i.e., rushed forward with fiery ardor).
segments.s142-g37: کمان بزه را به بازو فگند has the object کمان بزه را (marked by را) followed by the directional or locative phrase به بازو 'onto (her) arm' and the past verb فگند 'threw' at the end — 'she flung the ____ of the bow onto her arm.' Note the subject is omitted and understood from context and the next clause supplies the following action.
segments.s142-g38: سمندش برآمد به ابر بلند: سمندش ('his/her steed' — سمند + possessive clitic ش) is the subject, برآمد is the past 'rose/upreared' (verb following subject), and به ابر بلند is a prepositional phrase 'to the high/cloudy sky' (به with a genitive adjective بلند). Together: 'his steed reared and rose toward the lofty cloud.'
segments.s142-g39: سر نیزه را سوی سهراب کرد is a transitive clause: سر نیزه را (the spear's head, definite by را) + سوی سهراب (toward Sohrab) + کرد (did/made/turned) at the end. کرد here functions as a causative/lexical verb meaning 'turned/made (the spear's head face) toward Sohrab.'
segments.s142-g40: عنان و سنان را پر از تاب کرد is an object (عنان و سنان را 'the reins and the spear' marked by را) followed by a result/attribute phrase پر از تاب ('full of sway/force') and the verb کرد 'made/filled' at the end. پر از + noun is an adjectival construction meaning 'filled with X', and کرد is the causative 'made (them) full of (that).'
segments.s142-g121: چو begins a temporal clause meaning “when.” سهراب را is the definite object (را marks Sahrab as the thing perceived). دید is the past tense ‘saw’ and the prepositional phrase بر پشت زین (‘on the saddle’s back’) follows the verb, with بر + پشت + زین forming a locative: ‘on the back of the saddle.’
segments.s142-g122: چنین گفت is a formulaic phrase ‘thus he said.’ کای is a vocative particle meaning ‘O,’ followed by شاه (‘king’) as the head noun. ترکان چین is a genitive chain modifying شاه: ‘king of the Turks of China’ (modifier nouns follow the head).
segments.s142-g123: چرا is the question word ‘why.’ رنجه گشتی is a two-word predicate meaning ‘you have become distressed/afflicted’ (رنجه = to suffer; گشتی is the 2nd-person past/perfective form of گشتن used here with a resultant meaning). کنون is the time adverb ‘now,’ and بازگرد is an imperative ‘turn/return back.’
segments.s142-g124: This line uses a هم … هم … coordination (‘both … and …’). هم از آمدن = ‘(both) from coming,’ with از marking origin; هم ز دشت نبرد = ‘and from the plain’s battle,’ where ز is the poetic form of از and دشت نبرد (‘plain battle’) is a noun phrase. نبرد is a noun ‘battle.’
segments.s142-g125: بخندید is the past tense ‘he laughed.’ و simply links clauses. او را is the definite object ‘him’ (را marks it). به افسوس is an adverbial phrase ‘with regret/alas’ (به + interjection), and گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’ introducing the following content.
segments.s142-g126: که introduces reported speech (‘that’/‘…’). ترکان is the subject ‘the Turks.’ ز ایران uses the poetic z-form of از to mean ‘from/in Iran.’ نیابند is a present/subjunctive-style form of یافتن meaning ‘(they) will not/find not’ (negative, here ‘will not find’). جفت is a noun ‘match’ or ‘mate’ (object of نیابند).
segments.s142-g127: چنین بود is ‘thus it was.’ و links clauses. روزی here functions adverbially ‘one day’/‘some day’ and نبودت is a negative verb form addressing ‘you’ (‘you will not be/you will not have’ in archaic/poetic usage). ز من means ‘from me’ or ‘of me’ (i.e. no longer belonging to me).
segments.s142-g128: بدین (ب + دین in the manuscript segmentation) means ‘with/by this’; درد is ‘affliction/pain’ and غمگین مکن is the negative imperative construction ‘do not make (yourself) sad’ (مکن = do not). خویشتن is the reflexive object ‘yourself.’
segments.s142-g81: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now' setting the time. من is the 1st-person subject. گشایم is a 1st-person singular present/subjunctive/optative of گشودن/گشادن 'to open; to reveal' — here it has an hortative/conditional flavor 'if I were to unveil' or 'let me unveil'. چنین modifies the following noun phrase ('such'), and روی و موی is a coordinated noun phrase 'face and hair'. Word order is typical classical Persian: time (کنون) + subject (من) + verb (گشایم) + object (چنین روی و موی).
segments.s142-g82: سپاه تو is the noun phrase 'your army' (noun before possessive pronoun in classical order). گردد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of گشتن 'to become' (here 'will become' / 'becomes'). پر از is a fixed adjective phrase 'full of' followed by the object از + گفت‌وگو 'of conversation', so the clause means 'your army becomes full of conversation/talk'.
segments.s142-g83: که introduces a relative/clausal link 'that/who'. با + دختری indicates 'with a maiden' (با as the instrumental/companional preposition). او is the pronominal reference 'he/she' tied to the relative clause. به دشت places the action 'in/on the plain' (به with a locative sense), and نبرد is a noun 'battle/fight' used here to denote the fighting event. Word order: clause introducer + accompaniment + complement + location + event noun.
segments.s142-g84: بدین‌سان (here split as ب + دین + سان) is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this way/thus' (سان as 'so/thus'); به ابر اندر آورد گرد is a verbal phrase: به (into) + ابر (cloud) + اندر (in/within) + آورد (bring/cause) + گرد (an older verbal auxiliary/particle forming a resultive/passive-like phrase). Together it conveys 'thus caused/was brought into the cloud' or 'so he brought it about that (a) cloud formed/about'.
segments.s142-g85: نهانی is an adverb/adjective 'secretly / hidden'. بسازیم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative of ساختن 'to make/arrange' and here expresses a counterfactual or desirable action 'we should arrange / it would be better to arrange'. بهتر بود is a predicative phrase 'would have been better' (بود as past/copula used as modal/past). Together: 'It would have been better to arrange secretly.'
segments.s142-g86: خرد داشتن is a nominal verbal construction 'to have wisdom' (خرد = wisdom, داشتن = to have). کار مهتر بود places emphasis on the importance: کار 'affair/matter', مهتر 'greater/more important' (classical use meaning 'more significant'), and بود 'was' — so 'having wisdom / the wiser course was the more important matter'. Word order is noun-phrase + predicate, with مهتر functioning adjectivally/comparatively.
segments.s142-g87: ز بهر من is a classical circumstantial phrase 'for my sake / on my account' (ز here 'because/for', بهر 'for/for the sake of', من 'me'). آهو is the object 'the gazelle' (a term often used poetically for a beloved). ز هر سو means 'from every side' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every direction'). مخواه is a negative imperative 'do not seek / do not ask' (2nd-person negative), so the clause issues the prohibition 'do not look for the gazelle for me from every side.'
segments.s142-g88: میان دو صف is a locative phrase 'between two ranks/lines' (میان = between, دو صف = two battle-lines). برکشیده سپاه is a noun phrase 'the army drawn up' — برکشیده as a participle/adjective meaning 'raised/drawn up' describing سپاه (army). Together they describe the army formed up between two ranks.
segments.s142-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاه شد is a verbal phrase: آگاه (aware) + شد (past of شدن) = 'became aware' (past change of state). The noun phrase دختر گژدهم ('Gozhdaham's daughter') follows the verb by poetic inversion and lacks an explicit ezāfe — classical Persian often omits the connector in names/titles.
segments.s142-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. سالار آن انجمن is the subject phrase 'the leader/commander of that assembly' (آن = 'that'). گشت کم is a predicate: گشت (past of گشتن used as 'became') + کم (predicative adjective 'few/less/diminished') = 'had become diminished/grew few'.
segments.s142-g3: زنی بود is an existential/copular phrase 'there was a woman' (بود as past copula). برسان گردی سوار is a compact descriptive phrase: برسان ('bringing/sending') + گردی ('dust') + سوار ('rider/horseman') — poetically describing her as a mounted rider who raised dust as she rode.
segments.s142-g4: همیشه is an adverb 'always' or 'ever'. به جنگ اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/into the fighting, in close quarters combat' (اندرون = 'inner'). نامدار is the predicate adjective 'renowned' placed at the end to qualify the woman as 'ever-renowned in battle.'
segments.s142-g5: کجا here is a rhetorical/exclamatory opening (literary usage) leading into the equational clause. نام او بود گردآفرید is a simple past copular clause 'her name was Gordafarid' (بود = 'was'). The word order places the small interrogative/exclamatory particle before the equative statement.
segments.s142-g6: زمانه is 'the age/the times' as subject. ز مادر ('from mother') marks origin — 'from any mother' in sense of being born. چنین is 'such' and modifies the verb ناورید, an archaic negative past form meaning 'did not bring/produce.' Together: 'the age did not bring forth such a one from a mother.'
segments.s142-g7: چنان is an intensifier 'so/such'. ننگش is ننگ ('shame') + the third-person possessive suffix -ش = 'her shame'. آمد is past 'came/fell upon'. ز کار هجیر is an ablative phrase 'from Hajir's deed' (ز = 'because of/from'). The clause expresses cause: 'such shame came upon her because of Hajir's action.'
segments.s142-g8: که introduces the result clause 'so that/that'. شد is past 'became'. لاله رنگش is a compound image 'her tulip-color' (i.e., rosy complexion). به کردار is 'in the manner/appearance of' and قیر is 'tar/pitch' used metaphorically — the result: her rosy complexion turned pitch-black. Word order places the result predicate after the clause marker که.
segments.s142-g153: برآرم is 1st-person singular present/jussive from برآمدن (to rise/get up); here it carries a prospective sense 'I will rise / set out'. به + شبگیر is a prepositional temporal phrase ('at night/nightfall'), with شبگیر used adverbially ('by night'). ازین (archaic spelling of از این) + باره forms a genitival/nominal phrase 'from/about this matter' (باره = 'occasion, matter'); گرد here functions adverbially/verbally as 'around/about' or 'to go about', so the whole segment means roughly 'I will rise at night and go about this matter.'
segments.s142-g154: ببینند is 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive ('they may see' / 'let them see') and introduces a purpose/result clause. آسیب روز is a noun phrase 'the harm/damage of the day' (آسیب = harm; روز = day). نبرد here is not the noun 'battle' but the negative past 3rd-person singular of بردن ('did not take/seize'); in classical word order the verb comes at the end, so the subordinate clause reads 'that the day's harm did not take hold.' (Note the lexical ambiguity: written نبرد can also be read as the noun 'battle'.)
segments.s142-g17: This is an interrogative fragment: 'که' (= who/which) introduces the question, 'گردان' is the noun 'troops/regiment', and 'کدامند' is the copular interrogative 'which are (they)?' (classical SOV order: subject + predicate at the end). The conjunction 'و' then links the following noun phrase 'جنگ‌آوران' ('warriors') as part of the catalogue of persons being asked about.
segments.s142-g18: A coordinated noun phrase listing qualities/roles: 'دلیران' (the brave) and 'کارآزموده سران' (literally 'experienced leaders' — adjective + head). Adjectives and epithets precede the head noun in this construction, and the conjunction 'و' connects the two elements.
segments.s142-g19: 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; the following proper name 'سهراب شیراوژن' is the subject. 'او را' is the definite object marker plus pronoun 'him', and 'بدید' is the past tense verb 'saw' (classical SOV: subject — object — verb).
segments.s142-g20: Two coordinated actions of the same subject: 'بخندید' is past tense 'he laughed' followed by 'و' ('and'), then the clause 'لب را به دندان گزید' — 'لب را' (the lip, with object marker), 'به دندان' ('with/by the teeth'), and 'گزید' (here 'he bit' — past of 'گزیدن' meaning to bite).
segments.s142-g21: 'چنین گفت' is a formulaic 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said'). 'کامد' is an archaic/presentive verb 'comes' or 'will come', followed by the adverbial pair 'دگر باره' meaning 'again' (دگر = again, باره = time/occasion), and 'گور' is the noun 'grave'. The verb phrase placement is classical/poetic and compact.
segments.s142-g22: 'به دام' is the prepositional phrase 'into/into the snare of' (به = into/towards, دام = snare/trap). 'خداوند شمشیر و زور' is a genitival/title phrase 'the lord of sword and might' (خداوند used here as 'lord/sovereign'), with 'شمشیر و زور' ('sword and strength') specifying the epithet. Word order places the possessor phrase after the head noun 'دام' in compact poetic style.
segments.s142-g23: 'بپوشید' here functions as a past action 'he put on / covered himself with'; 'خفتان' in this context denotes the garment/covering (an older noun/verb form related to sleep/covering), and 'و' links the next action. 'بر سر نهاد' is a compact past construction meaning 'placed upon (his) head' ('بر' = on/upon, 'سر' = head, 'نهاد' = placed — past of نهادن).
segments.s142-g24: 'یکی' is the indefinite 'one/a certain' introducing the object 'ترگ چینی' (a piece/strip of Chinese cloth — 'ترگ' = a strip/piece, 'چینی' = Chinese). The simile 'به کردار باد' ('in the fashion/manner of the wind') modifies how it sat or fluttered: 'به' = by/in, 'کردار' = manner/behavior, 'باد' = wind.
segments.s142-g105: This is a past-tense verb clause with the object marked by the enclitic را before the verb; عِنان را بپیچید = “(she) twisted the reins.” The name گردآفرید (here written as two words) follows the verb as the subject, a common classical Persian word order (Verb — Subject). بپیچید is a past narrative form (ب‑ prefix + پیچید) meaning “twisted/turned.”
segments.s142-g106: سمند سرافراز بر دژ کشید is another past clause: the noun phrase (سمند سرافراز) is the object/topic and بر دژ is a prepositional phrase indicating direction/goal. کشید here is a past verb meaning “drew/led/dragged (to/onto)” (the provided lemma senses do not include this reading, so treated as lexical mismatch).
segments.s142-g107: همی رفت is an imperfect/continuous past-like narrative construction (همی + رفت) meaning “kept going” or “was going.” The conjunction و links to the next clause; سهراب is the subject of what follows, با او indicates accompaniment, and به هم (= together/meeting one another) emphasizes joint action.
segments.s142-g108: بیامد is a simple past tense verb meaning “came.” It takes the goal phrase به درگاه دژ گژدهم (to the threshold/gate of the fortress Gozhdaham); گژدهم is a proper name attached to دژ.
segments.s142-g109: در باره here functions as a locative phrase referring to the doorway/threshold area (در = door/gate). بگشاد is a past narrative form of گشودن (opened), and گردآفرید (written as گرد آفرید) is the subject placed after the verb. The verb is transitive: “(she) opened (the door).”
segments.s142-g110: تن خسته و بسته is a noun phrase “a body, tired and bound.” بر دژ is again a directional phrase (onto/into the fortress). کشید is the past verb meaning “drew/dragged/brought” (the supplied lemma senses do not capture this verbal meaning, so it is treated as a lexical mismatch).
segments.s142-g111: در دژ ببستند is a past plural clause where در (here: ‘at the gate/entrance’) + دژ marks location and ببستند is the 3rd-person plural past of بستن “they closed/shut.” The conjunction و links to غمگین شدند, a predicative phrase meaning “they became sad” (شدند = ‘became’).
segments.s142-g112: پر از غم is an adjectival/prepositional phrase “full of grief” (پر = full, از = of). دل و دیده خونین شدند is a coordinated nominal phrase with خونین (bloody/blood‑stained) as predicate adjective and شدند as the verb “became,” i.e. “their hearts and eyes became bloodied.”
segments.s142-g113: ز is the causal/prepositional particle 'because of' placed before the cause. آزار is a noun 'harm/injury'. گردآفرید و هجیر are proper names joined by و ('and'). Together this phrase means 'because of the harm to Gordafarid and Hejir.'
segments.s142-g114: پر از + noun is the idiom 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'). بودند is the past plural of 'to be' ('they were'). برنا و پیر is the coordinated subject 'the young and the old.' Word order is verb before subject (poetic), giving 'young and old were full of pain.'
segments.s142-g115: بگفتند is the past-tense 'they said' introducing speech. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone. نیکدل شیرزن is a two‑word epithet 'kind‑hearted (نیکدل) warrior‑woman (شیرزن).' The whole segment is the vocative clause 'They said, O kind‑hearted warrior‑woman.'
segments.s142-g116: پر از غم is 'full of sorrow' (پر + از + noun). بد here is an adverbial 'ill/woefully' and از تو marks 'on your account / because of you.' دل انجمن ('the heart of the assembly') is the topic: 'the assembly's heart is full of sorrow because of you.' Note classical word order keeps modifiers close together.
segments.s142-g117: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('that / for'). هم‌رزم جستی is second‑person past (جستی) with هم‌رزم 'companion‑in‑arms' — 'you went out/acted as a companion.' هم ... هم is the correlative 'both ... and ...' linking افسون (enchantment/charm) and رنگ (here 'appearance/guise' in a poetic sense).
segments.s142-g118: نیامد is the past negative 'did not come' (here meaning 'no ... befell'). ز کار تو = 'from / because of your deed.' بر + دوده ننگ is a prepositional phrase 'shame upon the soot' (i.e. no stain of disgrace befell the blackened/sooty face) — a poetic way to say 'no disgrace attached to you.' Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s142-g119: بخندید is the past 'laughed' (verb) and بسیار is the adverb 'greatly/much' modifying it. گرد آفرید here is the proper name گردآفرید split for meter; in poetry the name is separated into گرد + آفرید after the verb, so the segment reads 'Gordafarid laughed greatly.'
segments.s142-g120: به باره is a temporal phrase 'on that occasion / at that moment' (به + باره). برآمد is the past 'arose' and سپه is the subject 'host, army.' بنگرید is an archaic/imparative‑like form of 'to look/see' used narratively here as an exhortation or pictorial 'behold' (common in epic diction).
segments.s142-g137: نماند is the negative past/resultative verb (ن‑ + ماند) meaning “did not remain/was left not”; it governs the following nominal phrase which gives what did not remain. The word order is verb + numeral + adjective + prepositional source: “not‑remained one alive from your army.”
segments.s142-g138: ندانم is the present first‑person negative ‘I do not know.’ چه آید is an interrogative/subjunctive sequence meaning “what may come/happen”; آید is present/subjunctive/periphrastic future here. ز بد = “from/because of ill/bad (things)” and بر سرت is the prepositional phrase “upon your head / upon you.” Together: “I do not know what bad thing will come upon you.”
segments.s142-g139: دریغ آیدم is an impersonal/experiential expression = “it grieves me” or “I feel pity” (آیدم literally “comes to me”). کاین چنین is a fused demonstrative + particle meaning “that such/so” and introduces the following noun phrase یال و سفت (“mane and firmness/strength”), where یال is a concrete noun and سفت is an adjective/noun meaning “firm/tight/strong.”
segments.s142-g140: همی is a literary aspectual/adverbial particle (here marking the verbal phrase), از پلنگان = “from the leopards,” and بباید نهفت is a periphrastic obligation: بباید (must/ought) + نهفت (to hide/be concealed). The verb cluster expresses that these qualities must be hidden away from the leopards.
segments.s142-g141: ترا بهتر آید is an impersonal construction meaning “it is better for you” (ترا = ‘for you/you’, بهتر = ‘better’, آید = ‘it comes/suits’). که introduces the subordinate clause; فرمان کنی uses the present/subjunctive second‑person کنی to express an advised action: “that you give the command.”
segments.s142-g142: رخ نامور is a noun + adjective (“the famed face”), and سوی توران کنی is a verb phrase with سوی + proper noun as the goal (“toward Turan”) plus the second‑person subjunctive/optative کنی. The second کنی is coordinated with فرمان کنی: the speaker advises both ordering and turning one’s face toward Turan.
segments.s142-g143: نباشی here is a negative form of ‘to be’ directed at the second person and functions as a predictive/assertive ‘you will not be’ or cautionary ‘do not be’ depending on tone. بس ایمن = “sufficiently safe,” and به بازوی خویش = “by/with your own arm” (خویش = reflexive ‘one’s own’, formed from خوی + the pronominal suffix ش). Word order places the negated copula first for emphasis.
segments.s142-g144: This line is verb‑first (poetic inversion): خورد is a verb meaning ‘befalls/hits/strikes’ (here used to express that something may happen), followed by the subject گاو نادان (“an ignorant/foolish ox”). ز پهلوی خویش is a prepositional phrase “from your/its own flank” (خویش reflexive). The clause functions as a proverblike warning that even a foolish ox may wound from one’s own side.
segments.s142-g97: This is a verbless poetic clause where the subject (دو چشمش) and predicate nominals (گوزن / ابرو کمان) are juxtaposed without a copula — a common Persian poetic device. دو quantifies the following noun, and the possessive clitic -ش on چشم indicates 'her/his eye(s)'; the parallel structure links چشمش→گوزن and (دو) ابرو→کمان as metaphors.
segments.s142-g98: تو گفتی is a simple past (2nd person) 'you said'. همی is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning 'always/continually' here; بشکفد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive with the older b‑ prefix (ب + شکفد) meaning 'will open/bloom'. هر زمان is a temporal phrase 'every time/every moment' modifying بشکفد. The clause reports what 'you' said about their habitual blooming.
segments.s142-g99: بدو گفت is indirect speech: بدو is the allative clitic phrase 'to him' (بـ + او), followed by گفت (past 'said'). کاکنون is an emphatic/poetic form of 'now'. ازین is the preposition از + demonstrative این ('from this'); برمگرد is an imperative/command 'turn back/return' addressed to the listener (the verb is in the familiar imperative form).
segments.s142-g100: که introduces the content/reason clause ('for' / 'that'). دیدی is past 2nd-person 'you saw'. The sequence م + را here corresponds to the object 'مرا' split into clitic elements (the dataset lists them separately); را is the definite object marker attached to the pronoun. روزگار is used in the sense of 'fate' and نبرد is the finite verb meaning 'took/carried off' (an older form of برد used with the negative prefix n‑ in other contexts but here functioning as the past verb 'carried').
segments.s142-g101: برین باره is a compound: بر + این = 'for this' and باره 'occasion/affair'. The noun phrase دژ دل ('fortress of the heart') is a poetic genitive (no ezafe explicitly shown) and اندر is a preposition 'in/over'. مبند is the negative imperative 'do not close/bind' (م + بند — a prohibitive form addressing the addressee).
segments.s142-g102: که here gives the reason/explanation ('for/that'). این نیست is the copular negation 'this is not'. برتر is a comparative/adjectival predicate 'higher/superior'. ز is the poetic form of از ('than/from') used in comparisons. ابر بلند is an inverted noun + adjective 'lofty/tall cloud.'
segments.s142-g103: بپای آورد is a compound verbal expression: پای آوردن (here with the prefix بـ) means 'to bear/ to endure' or 'to come to pass for someone'; آورد is the light verb 'brought/caused'. زخم کوپال من is the object 'the Koopal wound of mine' (کوپال is a proper name or named weapon). Grammatically, the verb phrase is past tense (3rd person in form, but context yields first‑person experience 'I endured').
segments.s142-g104: This clause uses an archaic negative-past construction: نراند کسی parses as ن راند کسی → 'no one drove' (ن = negative prefix; راند = past of راندن 'to drive'; کسی = 'anyone' used with the negation to mean 'no one'). نیزه بر یال من is a prepositional phrase 'a spear upon/into my mane' with بر = 'on/upon' and یال 'mane'; من is the possessive 'my'. The result is 'no one thrust a spear into my mane.'
segments.s142-g129: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'truly/verily' placed at the clause start. که here is the linking/relative particle introducing the proposition. تو خود is an emphatic sequence ('you yourself'), where خود reinforces تو. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', giving origin, and ترکان is the plural noun 'the Turks'. نه‌ای is the present‑tense predicate meaning 'are not' (the negative copula with the present form of هست/هستی).
segments.s142-g130: که again introduces the second clause ('and/that'). جز is the excluding particle 'except' (here used idiomatically to mean 'only' or 'nothing but'). بآفرین is a compounded/formal word meaning 'with praise' or 'that which elicits praise' (poetic), and بزرگان is the genitive plural 'the great ones' or nobles; نه‌ای repeats the present 'you are not', completing the negated/exclusive predicate 'you are nothing except ...'. Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s142-g131: بدان is a fused prepositional phrase ('ب' + 'دان') functioning like 'with/in that' or 'as for that'. The following coordinated list زور و بازوی و آن کتف و یال enumerates physical/heroic attributes ('strength and arm, and that shoulder and mane'), with آن pointing to a specific shoulder already mentioned or known; یال ('mane') is a poetic image for crest/glory. The nouns are linked by و (and) in a typical Persian coordinate series.
segments.s142-g132: نداری here expresses lack/existence ('there is not' / 'you do not have'); کس is the indefinite 'anyone' (negated by نداری). از پهلوانان specifies the class 'among the champions', and همال is an archaic/poetic noun meaning 'peer/equal' or 'one of like rank'. The whole clause says that among heroes there does not exist anyone equal in the listed attributes.
segments.s142-g133: ولیکن is a formal conjunction 'but/yet'. چو here functions temporally as 'when'. آگاهی is the noun 'information/news' and آید is the present (or gnomic/future‑oriented) verb 'comes' — together آگاهی آید به شاه = 'news comes to the king' (قبلاً 'reaches the king'). The dative/goal به marks the recipient (شاه).
segments.s142-g134: که introduces the content of the news ('that ...'). آورد is the verb 'brings/has brought' (reporting verb), and گردی here is a poetic/archaic noun referring to a troop/host (contextually 'a force' rather than literal 'dust'); ز marks origin 'from', توران is the place name 'Turan', and سپاه is 'army/host'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic rhythm (verb before its noun phrase).
segments.s142-g135: This is a coordinated subject 'شهنشاه و رستم' ('the emperor and Rostam') followed by بجنبد, a present/future‑oriented verb meaning 'will move/are roused' (poetic present used to indicate imminent action). ز جای is the prepositional phrase 'from their place(s)'. The verb precedes the locative phrase for metrical/poetic order.
segments.s142-g136: شما is the addressed plural/polite 'you'. با marks the adversary/agent 'against/with' and introduces تهمتن (Tahmatan, an epithet of a mighty warrior). ندارید is the present 'you do not have/possess' (here idiomatically negative), and پای literally 'foot' is used idiomatically ('have no foot') meaning 'have no footing / cannot stand (against him)'. The whole segment says 'you cannot stand against Tahmatan.'
segments.s142-g89: کنون is a sentence‑initial temporal adverb 'now'. لشکر و دژ is a coordinated subject ('army and fortress'). به فرمان is a prepositional phrase 'under/at the command (of)'; تست here is an archaic poetic copula/possessive ('توست' ≈ 'is yours') forming 'under your command' — in classical usage the copula can be attached after the noun phrase.
segments.s142-g90: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not/should not' that governs the following verbal phrase. برین is the fused sequence بر + این ('بر این' = 'upon/on this'); word order places this locative/emphatic phrase before the action. آشتی جنگ جست is the verbal core: آشتی ('truce/reconciliation') and جنگ ('war') appear together with جست (from جستن) meaning 'to seek' — so the whole clause says 'one should not seek war upon this truce.'
segments.s142-g91: A list of three nouns (دژ و گنج و دژبان) serving as the subject. سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entirely' that modifies the predicate. تراست is the fused copula 'تو + است' (literary/poetic) meaning 'it is yours' — predicate follows the subject as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s142-g92: چو آیی is a subordinate temporal clause 'when you come' (چو = 'when', آیی = 2nd‑person present/subjunctive of 'come'). بدان is the prepositional phrase 'to/unto that' (here written as ب + دان in the split data). ساز appears here as an imperative/verb 'make/arrange' (poetic form identical to the noun in the lemma list) and کت is its object 'a coat/garment'; دل هواست is a small clause 'the heart is desire/longing' (دل = 'heart', هوا = 'desire', ست = copula 'is'). The clause as a whole has the sense 'when you come there, fashion it as a garment — the heart desires it.'
segments.s142-g93: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. رخساره is the direct object 'countenance/face' and بنمود is the past tense verb 'showed/revealed' (3rd‑person past). سهراب را is the marked object/beneficiary with را (here indicating the person affected: 'to Sohrab' or 'for Sohrab'). Word order places object(s) before the verb, which is typical in classical poetry.
segments.s142-g94: ز خوشاب is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of the pleasant drink/sweet draught' (ز = 'from/because of', خوشاب = 'sweet/pleasant water or wine'). بگشاد here is a prefixed past verb meaning 'opened/split' (ب‑ + گشاد(ن)), taking عناب را ('the jujube') as its object (عناب را with را marking definiteness). The clause reads literally 'from the sweet drink he opened the jujube.'
segments.s142-g95: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain' introducing the noun phrase. بوستان بد is 'a (particular) garden' with بد as a qualifying adjective (poetic word order: noun + adjective). در اندر بهشت is a locative phrase with two synonymous locatives (در, اندر) used poetically to mean 'in/within Paradise.'
segments.s142-g96: به بالای او is a locative phrase 'to/at its top' (به + بالای + او). سرو دهقان is a noun phrase 'a cypress (and) a peasant' but here functions as 'a peasant planted a cypress'; نکشت is the verbal element in past tense meaning 'planted' (archaic poetic past). The sequence follows the typical classical order where locative/NP precedes the verb.
segments.s142-g65: شگفت آمدش is a compact classical construction meaning “it came as wonder to him” → “he was astonished”; آمدش is آمد + clitic -ش (‘to him’). گفت is the main verb ‘said’ introducing reported speech. از ایران سپاه is the phrase quoted after گفت: از = 'from', ایران = the land/realm, سپاه = 'an army/host'. Word order is poetic but functions like: (With) astonishment it came to him; he said: 'an army from Iran...'.
segments.s142-g66: چنین is a demonstrative/determiner 'such' (emphatic); دختر is the subject 'maiden/young woman'; آید is the present-tense 3rd-person verb 'comes' (classical/poetic present). به + آوردگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the battlefield' (به here = 'towards/into'). The clause reads literally 'such a maiden comes to the battlefield.'
segments.s142-g67: سواران جنگی is a noun phrase 'war‑horsemen' or 'military riders' (سواران + the adjective/epithet جنگی). به‌روز نبرد is a temporal phrase 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز = 'on the day / of the day', classical idiom), specifying when these riders act.
segments.s142-g68: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily / behold'. آرند is the classical 3rd‑person plural present 'they bring' / 'they raise'; گرد here is the direct object meaning 'dust' (a cloud/whirl of dust). The prepositional cluster به ابر اندر (literally 'into a cloud/in cloud') shows direction/goal: they 'bring (or whirl) dust into cloud(s)' — poetic word order places adverbials before the verb.
segments.s142-g69: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from'; فتراک is 'saddle‑cloth' (the place where the rope is kept); بگشاد here is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'he opened/unfolded' (from گشادن); پیچان is an adjective 'coiling/twisting' modifying کمند 'lasso/snare'. The sequence means he drew/unfolded a coiled lasso from his saddle‑cloth.
segments.s142-g70: بینداخت is a past tense verb 'he threw/cast (it)'. و آمد is 'and he came/approached' (و = 'and'); میانش = میان + clitic -ش ('in its middle' / 'into its midst'), where the clitic refers back to کمند (the lasso). ببند is an imperative 'bind!' / 'fasten!' addressed to another (second person). So: he cast it and stepped forward, saying/commanding 'bind it by its middle.'
segments.s142-g71: بدو is a dative phrase 'to him' (بد + دative clitic sense), so بدو گفت = 'he said to him'. کز is a contraction of که + از meaning here 'that / from' introducing the subordinate object clause 'that you seek release from me.' من is 'me/I' (the source of release), رهایی is 'release/freedom', and مجوی is the negative jussive/imperative form 'do not seek' (classical present subjunctive/imperative).
segments.s142-g72: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. جنگ جویی is a verbal phrase where جویی (present form) means 'you seek' and جنگ is its object 'war/battle' → 'why do you seek war?'. تو‌ای is vocative 'you — O ...' (تو + ای for address) and ماهروی ('moon‑faced') is a vocative epithet meaning 'beautiful, moon‑faced one'. Persian omits a copula here; the present sense is conveyed by جویی.
segments.s142-g145: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when." بشنید is the past tense (3rd singular) of شنیدن "to hear," so the clause reads "when Sohrab heard." ننگ آمدش is an idiomatic simple past: ننگ "shame" as subject and آمدش (آمد + -ش enclitic) "came to him/ befell him." The clitic -ش marks third person (to him).
segments.s142-g146: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause here ("for/that"). آسان is an adjective "easy," and همی is an archaic/poetic particle (here emphatic/attenuating: "indeed/just/so") often used before verbs or predicates in Ferdowsi. دژ "fortress" is the subject of the following verbal phrase به چنگ آمدش: به + چنگ آمدن is an idiom "to come into (one's) grasp/hand" (چنگ = grasp/claw) and آمدش again uses the enclitic -ش "to him."
segments.s142-g147: به زیر دژ is a prepositional locative phrase "under/beneath the fortress" (به + زیر + دژ). اندر is an older preposition meaning "in/within." یکی جای بود is an existential past construction: یکی "a certain/one" + جای "place/spot" + بود (past of بودن) "was." Word order (locative first, then 'in', then indefinite noun + بود) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s142-g148: کجا here is a relative/adverbial "where." دژ بدان جای is a compressed classical phrase meaning "the fortress on that place" (بدان = ب + دان; ب functions as a locative/prefix here). بر پای بود: بر 'on/upon' + پای 'base/foot (of)' + بود 'was' — literally, "the fortress was upon that base." (Note: دان as listed senses doesn't fit this usage; treat بدان as a single particle meaning 'that'/'on that'.)
segments.s142-g149: به تاراج داد is a causative/result phrase: به + تاراج (pillage) + داد (gave) -> "gave to pillage" idiomatically "laid waste/plundered." آن همه بوم means "all that land/country" (آن 'that' + همه 'all' + بوم 'homeland'). و رست: و 'and' + رست (past of رستن) here means "he escaped/was delivered." The sequence reports two successive actions.
segments.s142-g150: به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase "suddenly/at once" (به + یکبارگی). دست بد را is the direct object with را marking: دست 'hand' used idiomatically for "the guilty/evil hand" or "ill deed," modified by بد 'bad/evil.' بشست is past tense "he washed." The combined clause conveys a swift, figurative act of "washing" (cleansing) his hand after the deed.
segments.s142-g151: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening "thus he said." کامروز is an emphatic temporal adverb "this very day/today." بیگاه here means "at dawn/untimely" and is placed after the temporal noun; گشت is the past of گشتن used as a copula/auxiliary meaning "it turned/it became" — the clause states when or how things turned out. (Note: the lemma senses list for گفت did not include the usual verbal sense 'said'; the gloss treats it as the speech verb.)
segments.s142-g152: ز is the poetic preposition "from/because of." پیگارمان is پیگار + ـمان (our battle/struggle). دست کوتاه گشت is an idiom: دست here means power/ability, کوتاه "short" used figuratively with گشت "became/was cut off" — "our hand (power) was cut short/was defeated." The sentence describes loss of strength in the struggle.
segments.s142-g73: نیامد is a past negative—'did not come'—used here to state that something did not occur. بدامم is بدام + ـم, literally 'into my trap' (بدام = 'into the snare', ـم = 'my'). بسان is a simile particle 'like/as', followed by تو 'you' (vocative/second person) and گور 'grave' — the phrase literally 'a grave like you did not come into my trap.' Word order places the verb before the phrase describing what did not come.
segments.s142-g74: ز is the preposition 'from'. چنگم is چنگ ('grasp, claw') plus possessive ـم 'my', yielding 'my grasp'. رهایی means 'release/freedom'. نیابی here is the 2nd-person negative result/subjunctive form 'you will not obtain/find' (expressing lack of escape). مشور is the noun 'counsel/consultation' used elliptically (an admonition: 'do not seek counsel' or 'do not hope for counsel').
segments.s142-g75: بدانست is the perfect/past 'he/she/it knew' or 'realized'. کاویخت here functions as a verbal root meaning 'sought/explored' (or can be read as the verbal notion of having searched); گردآفرید is the proper name 'Gordafarid' placed after the verb for emphasis, so the clause reads roughly 'Gordafarid realized/understood.'
segments.s142-g76: مر is the dative 'to me'/'for me'. آن را is the demonstrative object 'that' with the object marker را. جز از = 'except by/from'. چاره درمان is a collocation 'a remedy/means of cure' (چاره = remedy/expedient, درمان = cure/treatment). ندید is past 'saw' used idiomatically as 'found' (i.e. 'she saw/found no remedy'). The whole clause: 'to me/for that, she saw no remedy except by a stratagem.'
segments.s142-g77: بدو = 'to him/unto him' (prepositional/directional). روی بنمود is an idiom 'to show the face' = 'to turn (one's) face' (بنمود is past 'showed/turned'). و گفت links the action to speech: 'and (she) said'. ای is the vocative particle 'O', and دلیر is the vocative noun 'brave one.'
segments.s142-g78: میان دلیران = 'among the brave' (میان = among/between; دلیران = plural 'the brave'). به کردار is a prepositional expression 'in the manner of / by the conduct of' (به + کردار), where به functions adverbially ('-ly/by'). شیر is the simile 'lion' — together 'among the brave, in the manner/behavior of a lion.'
segments.s142-g79: دو لشکر = 'two armies' (numeral + noun). نظاره is a verbal-noun meaning 'watching/observation' used here with force of 'looked on' (classical Persian often uses a noun where modern English uses a verb). برین is بر + این (here 'بر' = 'on/upon' and 'ین' = archaic form 'this'), so برین جنگ ما = 'upon this battle of ours' (جنگ ما = 'our battle').
segments.s142-g80: برین again = 'upon/at this'. گرز و شمشیر و آهنگ ما is a list of the instruments and motion of the fight: گرز (mace/club), شمشیر (sword), and آهنگ ما — آهنگ here = 'pace/motion' or 'onset/attack' (i.e. our forward movement). The phrase catalogs what the armies were watching: our maces, swords, and our charge.
